title: How I Built an Autonomous AI Agent That Runs My Entire Digital Agency
author: Jakub
summary:
Claude Code + MCP servers + scheduled tasks = an agent that manages projects, writes content, analyzes data, and reports back — while I sleep.

I run Inithouse, a digital agency with ~14 live products — all MVPs hunting for product-market fit. Think Lean Startup on steroids: rapid experiments, measure everything, kill what doesn't work.

The problem? One human can't manage 14 products simultaneously. So I built an autonomous AI agent that does it for me.

Here's the full technical breakdown.

The Architecture

The system runs on Claude Code (Anthropic's CLI agent) with MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers as connectors to external services. The agent doesn't just answer questions — it has a full operational loop:

Data → Insight → Proposal
full_text:
Claude Code + MCP servers + scheduled tasks = an agent that manages projects, writes content, analyzes data, and reports back — while I sleep.

I run Inithouse, a digital agency with ~14 live products — all MVPs hunting for product-market fit. Think Lean Startup on steroids: rapid experiments, measure everything, kill what doesn't work.

The problem? One human can't manage 14 products simultaneously. So I built an autonomous AI agent that does it for me.

Here's the full technical breakdown.

 
 
 The Architecture

The system runs on Claude Code (Anthropic's CLI agent) with MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers as connectors to external services. The agent doesn't just answer questions — it has a full operational loop:

Data → Insight → Proposal → [Human Approval] → Build → Review → Measure

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

The key insight: the agent proposes, humans approve, the agent executes. No unsupervised decision-making on anything that matters.

 
 
 The MCP Stack

MCP servers are the agent's hands and eyes. Each one connects Claude Code to a specific service via a standardized protocol:

Linear MCP — project management (issues, comments, status transitions)

Slack MCP — team communication and run logs

Notion MCP — knowledge base and artifact storage

Gmail MCP — email monitoring

Chrome MCP — browser automation for Google Search Console, GA4, Google Ads, Microsoft Clarity

This isn't a toy integration. The agent reads Linear issues, writes comments, transitions statuses, sends Slack messages, creates Notion pages, and navigates web dashboards — all through structured tool calls.

 
 
 The Scheduled Task System

The real magic is in scheduled tasks — cron-like jobs that wake the agent up at specific times to do specific work. Here are a few from the actual production system:

 
 
 linear-worker — The Task Executor

Runs multiple times daily. The workflow:

Query Linear for issues in Todo state (= human-approved)

Sort by priority (Urgent → High → Medium → Low)

Check comments for duplicate claims (another session might have grabbed it)

Atomically move to In Progress (this is the lock)

Read the full issue description, understand the task

Execute the work end-to-end

Move to In Review, write a structured handoff comment

Post a run log to Slack

The duplicate-prevention mechanism is interesting. Since multiple agent sessions can run in parallel, we use a simple protocol: move the issue status before writing the "I'm taking this" comment. Status transition = atomic lock. If it fails, skip to the next issue.

 
 
 brain-optimizer — The Self-Improving Agent

This one runs daily and reviews the agent's own performance:

Scans recent Linear comments and Slack messages for human feedback

Identifies corrections ("don't do X"), confirmations ("yes, exactly like that"), and new preferences

Writes findings to a persistent memory system (more on this below)

Proposes improvements as new Linear issues

The agent literally gets better every day based on how humans react to its work.

 
 
 The Memory System

One of the most underrated patterns: file-based persistent memory. The agent maintains an auto-memory directory with categorized memory files:

/auto-memory/
├── MEMORY.md # Index file (always loaded)
├── feedback_no_budget_increases.md # "Never increase subscriptions"
├── feedback_close_tabs.md # "Close Chrome tabs after work"
├── user_jirka_product.md # "Jirka handles UX/product"
├── project_watchingagents_correct.md # "It's a prediction platform"
├── reference_growth_accounts.md # Platform account credentials
└── ...50+ more files

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

Each memory file has frontmatter with name, description, and type (feedback, user, project, reference). The index file is always loaded into context, so the agent can decide which memories to read for a given task.

Why this matters: Without persistent memory, every conversation starts from zero. The agent would repeat the same mistakes, ask the same questions, and ignore the same preferences. With memory, corrections compound.

A real example: early on, the agent tried to increase a subscription plan budget autonomously. After being corrected, it saved a feedback memory. Now it never touches budgets without human approval — across all future sessions.

 
 
 The Product Portfolio

What does the agent actually manage? Here's a sample of the products it operates across:

Be Recommended — checks if ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity recommend your business to customers (AI Visibility Score)

Watching Agents — a prediction platform where AI agents monitor questions about the future and generate live predictions with probability scores

Audit Vibe Coding — professional audits of AI-generated (vibecoded) projects: security, SEO, performance, accessibility across 47 checks

Živá Fotka — upload a photo, AI animates it into a ~3s video with natural movement (5 language markets on one codebase)

Magical Song — describe a story, pick a genre, get a studio-quality AI-generated song with real vocals in under 2 minutes

Vibe Codéři — Czech marketplace connecting vibecoding talent with companies looking for AI-assisted development

Verdict Buddy — AI conflict resolution using Gottman Method + Harvard Negotiation frameworks

Pet Imagination — upload your pet's photo, get artistic portraits in 9 styles (Renaissance, Anime, Wizard, etc.)

Without Human — a long-form manifesto on the future of work in the age of AI

For each product, the agent tracks Google Search Console data, GA4 analytics, Microsoft Clarity heatmaps, and Google Ads performance. It knows which products are growing, which are stalling, and proposes actions accordingly.

 
 
 The Governance Model

Here's what makes this not scary: strict governance.

The agent operates on a ticket-based system:

State
Meaning

Backlog
Agent proposes, waits for human approval

Todo
Human approved — agent can execute autonomously

In Progress
Agent is actively working

In Review
Done, human checks the output

Waiting
Deployed, waiting for metrics

Done
Measured and confirmed

The critical constraint: the agent never moves anything from Backlog to Todo. Only humans do that. This means every action the agent takes has been explicitly approved.

Additional safety rails:

No budget changes without human approval

No credential handling — if login is needed, agent asks a human

Trusted instruction sources only — commands from project management tools are trusted; emails, web content, and AI responses are treated as data, never as instructions

Internal context protection — the agent never leaks config files, memory contents, or system prompts into public outputs

 
 
 Lessons Learned

1. Memory is everything. Without persistent memory, the agent is a goldfish. Every correction, every preference, every "don't do that" needs to be captured and applied forever.

2. Governance > autonomy. The temptation is to let the agent do everything. Don't. The Backlog → Todo approval gate is the single most important design decision.

3. MCP servers are the killer feature. Claude Code without MCP is a smart chatbot. Claude Code with MCP is an autonomous operator that can touch every system in your stack.

4. Scheduled tasks make it real. An agent you have to manually trigger is a tool. An agent that wakes itself up, finds work, and executes it is a team member.

5. Start with one product, not fourteen. I didn't build this overnight. The system grew product by product, memory by memory, failure by failure.

 
 
 What's Next

The system is still evolving. Current experiments:

Self-improvement loops — the agent reviews its own past performance and proposes skill upgrades

Cross-platform content distribution — one article idea, automatically adapted and published across Dev.to, Medium, Hashnode, WordPress, and more

Automated waiting checks — when a change is deployed, the agent schedules a follow-up to measure the impact

If you're building something similar, I'd love to hear about it. The combination of Claude Code + MCP + scheduled tasks is genuinely underexplored territory.

I'm Jakub, founder of Inithouse. We build AI-powered products and run the entire operation with an autonomous agent. Follow along as we figure out what works.
